name: Critical Observation
instructions: You will answer 6 critical observation questions. Some present a scenario and ask you to identify all the issues; others ask you to write your analysis. Select ALL answers you believe are correct on multi-select questions. Timers are running but hidden — you will receive a warning when time is almost up. You cannot go back to previous questions.

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] Review the order confirmation and approval workflow described below. Identify ALL issues, gaps, or contradictions present in the process.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: A company uses an automated order confirmation system. When a customer places an order over $500, the system sends an immediate confirmation email stating the order is approved. However, orders over $500 also require a manager's manual approval before fulfillment. The confirmation email contains a delivery date estimate. The approval process takes 1–3 business days. There is no follow-up communication if the order is rejected after confirmation.

options.text: [PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The order confirmation email states the order is approved before manager approval has occurred
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The delivery date estimate is provided before the approval delay is factored in
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The system font used in confirmation emails is not brand-compliant
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: There is no communication path for notifying the customer if the order is rejected
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The 1–3 business day approval window contradicts the immediate confirmation of approval
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The order confirmation email does not mention the pending approval requirement

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] Review the employee scheduling system described below. Identify ALL issues, gaps, or contradictions in the design.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: A retail store uses an automated scheduling system. Employees submit availability weekly by Thursday. The system generates the next week's schedule on Friday and posts it. Employees can swap shifts with any colleague without manager approval. The system enforces a maximum of 40 hours per week per employee. However, shift swaps are not validated against the 40-hour cap. Part-time employees are in the same scheduling pool as full-time employees despite having different hour limits.

options.text: [PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Shift swaps bypass the 40-hour weekly maximum enforcement
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The schedule background color is not configurable per department
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Part-time and full-time employees share a scheduling pool without differentiated hour caps
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: There is no manager oversight on shift swaps that could create compliance issues
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: One-day turnaround between availability deadline and schedule posting leaves no time for conflict resolution
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The system does not account for employees who fail to submit availability by the Thursday deadline

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] Review the customer satisfaction survey process described below. Identify ALL issues, gaps, or contradictions in the approach.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: A SaaS company sends a satisfaction survey to all customers 24 hours after they contact support. The survey asks customers to rate their experience on a 1–5 scale and includes an open comment box. Results are averaged monthly and reported to leadership. The survey is only sent via email. Customers who contact support multiple times in a week receive multiple surveys. The survey does not distinguish between resolved and unresolved tickets. Response rates hover around 12%.

options.text: [PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Multiple surveys sent to repeat contacts creates survey fatigue and skews data
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No distinction between resolved and unresolved tickets makes the satisfaction data unreliable
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The survey button color does not match the company brand guidelines
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Email-only distribution excludes customers who prefer other communication channels
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: A 12% response rate introduces significant non-response bias into the monthly averages
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Monthly averaging obscures trends and spikes in dissatisfaction that require immediate action

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] Review the automated invoice processing workflow described below. Identify ALL issues, gaps, or contradictions in the system.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: A company uses an automated invoice processing system. Invoices received via email are scanned by OCR and matched against purchase orders. Matched invoices are auto-approved if under $10,000. Invoices over $10,000 require manual review. The OCR system has a 94% accuracy rate. Duplicate invoice numbers are flagged, but invoices from different vendors with the same amount are not cross-checked. The system processes invoices in batch every 4 hours. Payment terms start from the invoice date, not the processing date. There is no escalation path for invoices that fail OCR parsing.

options.text: [PLACEHOLDER] Issue: 94% OCR accuracy means 6% of invoices may have incorrect data auto-approved under $10,000
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No escalation path for OCR parse failures creates a black hole for unprocessable invoices
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: 4-hour batch processing introduces delays that could cause payment term violations
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Payment terms starting from invoice date rather than processing date disadvantages the company
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Same-amount invoices from different vendors are not cross-checked, enabling potential fraud
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No validation step between OCR output and auto-approval for invoices under $10,000
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The invoice PDF rendering quality could be improved for archival purposes

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] Review the meeting room reservation system described below. Identify ALL issues, gaps, or contradictions in the design.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: A corporate office uses an online meeting room reservation system. Employees can book rooms up to 2 weeks in advance. Bookings are first-come, first-served with no priority levels. Recurring meetings can be set up for up to 6 months. The system allows double-booking if one reservation is marked as 'tentative.' Rooms are auto-released if no one checks in within 15 minutes, but there is no check-in mechanism integrated into the system. Cancelled meetings do not automatically free the room in the calendar. VIP executives and interns have identical booking privileges.

options.text: [PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Double-booking allowed for tentative reservations creates scheduling conflicts
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Auto-release after 15 minutes is unenforceable without an integrated check-in mechanism
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Cancelled meetings not auto-freeing rooms creates phantom bookings that waste space
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Recurring meetings for 6 months with a 2-week booking window creates a policy contradiction
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No priority levels means critical client meetings compete equally with informal gatherings
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No mechanism to handle conflicts when a tentative booking becomes confirmed against an existing booking
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The room listing page should display floor plans for better navigation

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] Review the product return policy described below. Identify ALL issues, gaps, or contradictions in the policy.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: An online retailer offers a 30-day return policy. Customers must initiate returns through the website portal. The portal requires uploading a photo of the item. Refunds are processed within 5–7 business days after the item is received at the warehouse. The policy states 'all items are eligible for return' but the FAQ page lists electronics and perishables as non-returnable. Return shipping is free for defective items, but the system does not ask the reason for the return until after the shipping label is generated. Items on clearance are marked as 'final sale' only on the product page, not at checkout.

options.text: [PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The return shipping label font size is too small to scan at some facilities
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Contradiction between 'all items eligible' policy and FAQ exclusions for electronics and perishables
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Return reason collected after label generation prevents conditional free shipping for defective items
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Clearance 'final sale' marking only on product page, not at checkout, creates disputes
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Photo upload requirement may prevent returns for items with non-visible defects
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No mechanism to validate that the returned item matches the order before processing refund

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] Review the automated hiring pipeline described below. Identify ALL issues, gaps, or contradictions in the process.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: A tech company uses an automated hiring pipeline. Resumes are parsed by an AI system that scores candidates 0–100 based on keyword matching. Candidates scoring above 70 are auto-invited to a coding assessment. The coding assessment is timed at 90 minutes and auto-graded. Candidates scoring above 80% on the assessment are scheduled for interviews. The AI resume parser was trained on data from the company's last 5 years of hires. The system does not accommodate candidates who request accessibility accommodations for the timed assessment. Rejection emails are sent instantly for candidates scoring below 70, with no human review. There is no appeals process. The keyword matching gives higher scores to candidates who list more technologies, regardless of depth of experience.

options.text: [PLACEHOLDER] Issue: AI trained on historical hire data perpetuates existing biases in hiring patterns
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Keyword quantity over quality scoring rewards resume padding over genuine expertise
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No accessibility accommodations for timed assessment creates legal and ethical liability
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Instant rejection below 70 with no human review eliminates potentially strong candidates with non-traditional backgrounds
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No appeals process violates fair hiring practice standards and reduces candidate trust
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The 70-point threshold is applied uniformly without adjusting for role-specific requirements
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The career portal should have a darker color scheme for better readability

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] A product manager has written the brief below for a real-time sales dashboard. Review the brief and describe all the issues, gaps, missing requirements, or unstated assumptions you can identify.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: Brief excerpt — 'Build a real-time sales dashboard that shows revenue by region, top-selling products, and sales rep performance. The dashboard should update every 5 seconds. It needs to support 500 concurrent users. Data comes from the existing CRM. Launch deadline is 3 weeks. The design should be clean and modern. Mobile support is nice to have.'

constraints.placeholder_text: Describe the issues, gaps, or assumptions you observe...

criteria: [PLACEHOLDER] Identifies that 5-second refresh with 500 concurrent users has significant infrastructure implications
[PLACEHOLDER] Notes the absence of defined data latency and staleness requirements
[PLACEHOLDER] Identifies missing access control and role-based visibility requirements
[PLACEHOLDER] Recognizes that 'clean and modern' is not an actionable design specification
[PLACEHOLDER] Questions the 3-week deadline feasibility given the technical requirements
[PLACEHOLDER] Notes that 'nice to have' mobile support is vague and could become a scope creep risk

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] A warehouse operations lead has described the low-stock alert system below. Review the description and identify all the issues, gaps, missing requirements, or unstated assumptions.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: Description excerpt — 'When inventory for any SKU drops below 50 units, the system sends an email alert to the purchasing team. The team then manually creates a purchase order. Alerts are sent once per day in a batch at 6 AM. The threshold of 50 units applies to all SKUs regardless of sales velocity. Suppliers have varying lead times from 2 days to 6 weeks. The system tracks current stock but not incoming shipments or pending orders. There is no integration with the sales forecast system.'

constraints.placeholder_text: Describe the issues, gaps, or assumptions you observe...

criteria: [PLACEHOLDER] Identifies that a flat 50-unit threshold ignores vastly different sales velocities across SKUs
[PLACEHOLDER] Notes that daily batch alerts at 6 AM could miss critical stockouts occurring during the day
[PLACEHOLDER] Recognizes the gap between 2-day and 6-week supplier lead times and the uniform alert threshold
[PLACEHOLDER] Identifies that not tracking incoming shipments or pending orders leads to duplicate purchasing
[PLACEHOLDER] Notes the lack of sales forecast integration means the system is purely reactive, not predictive
[PLACEHOLDER] Questions the manual purchase order creation as a bottleneck in the restocking process
[PLACEHOLDER] Identifies missing escalation path for SKUs that remain below threshold after initial alert

levels.label: Exceptional

levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] The HR director has announced the performance review cycle change described below. Review the announcement and identify all the issues, gaps, risks, unstated assumptions, and missing considerations.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: Announcement excerpt — 'Effective next quarter, we are moving from annual to quarterly performance reviews. Managers will complete reviews for all direct reports within a 2-week window at the end of each quarter. Reviews will use a new 1–5 rating scale replacing the current narrative format. Compensation adjustments will be tied to quarterly ratings. The new system will be implemented company-wide simultaneously. HR will provide a 1-hour training session for managers. The review platform vendor has been selected but integration with payroll is still in progress. Employees were informed via a company-wide email last week.'

constraints.placeholder_text: Describe the issues, gaps, or assumptions you observe...

criteria: [PLACEHOLDER] Identifies that quarterly compensation adjustments tied to ratings create volatility and short-term thinking
[PLACEHOLDER] Notes that a 2-week review window for all direct reports creates an intense workload burden on managers
[PLACEHOLDER] Recognizes that replacing narrative format with numeric scale loses qualitative context and nuance
[PLACEHOLDER] Questions whether 1-hour training is sufficient for a fundamental change in review methodology
[PLACEHOLDER] Identifies the risk of launching company-wide without a pilot phase to identify issues
[PLACEHOLDER] Notes the payroll integration being incomplete while the launch date is set creates a dependency risk
[PLACEHOLDER] Identifies that employee communication via email only, with no feedback channel, lacks change management rigor
[PLACEHOLDER] Recognizes the absence of a transition plan for how current-cycle reviews will be handled during the switchover

levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] The operations team has proposed the document approval policy described below. Review the policy and identify all the issues, gaps, risks, unstated assumptions, and missing considerations.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: Policy excerpt — 'All external-facing documents must be approved before distribution. Documents are submitted to a shared inbox for review. The legal team reviews within 3 business days. Marketing reviews within 2 business days. Both approvals are required. If either team rejects, the document is returned to the author with comments. There is no version tracking during the review cycle. Approved documents are stored in a shared drive folder. Urgent documents can bypass the process with VP approval, but there is no definition of what qualifies as urgent. The policy applies to all departments but was drafted without input from Sales, Engineering, or Customer Success. There is no periodic review schedule for the policy itself.'

constraints.placeholder_text: Describe the issues, gaps, or assumptions you observe...

criteria: [PLACEHOLDER] Identifies that sequential legal + marketing review creates a 5-day minimum bottleneck for every document
[PLACEHOLDER] Notes the lack of version tracking during review cycles creates confusion about which draft is current
[PLACEHOLDER] Recognizes that the shared inbox model has no assignment, prioritization, or SLA tracking mechanism
[PLACEHOLDER] Questions the undefined 'urgent' bypass criteria as a loophole that undermines the entire policy
[PLACEHOLDER] Identifies that drafting the policy without cross-departmental input reduces buy-in and may miss critical workflows
[PLACEHOLDER] Notes the absence of a periodic policy review schedule means the process will become outdated
[PLACEHOLDER] Identifies the lack of an audit trail for approvals and rejections creates compliance risk
[PLACEHOLDER] Recognizes that storing approved documents in a shared drive without access controls or retention policies is a governance gap

questions.prompt: [PLACEHOLDER] Review the support channel consolidation plan described below. Identify ALL issues, gaps, or contradictions in the proposal.
questions.context: [PLACEHOLDER] Scenario: A company currently handles customer support through email, live chat, phone, and social media, each managed by separate teams. The proposal is to consolidate all channels into a single ticketing platform within 6 weeks. All agents will be cross-trained to handle any channel. The existing knowledge base will be migrated but not updated. Current SLAs differ by channel: email is 24 hours, chat is 2 minutes, phone is immediate, social media is 4 hours. The new system will apply a uniform 4-hour SLA across all channels. Historical ticket data from each channel will not be migrated. The project has no dedicated change management or communication plan for customers.

options.text: [PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Uniform 4-hour SLA degrades chat and phone response expectations from minutes/immediate to hours
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Cross-training all agents on all channels in 6 weeks risks quality degradation during the transition
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Not migrating historical ticket data eliminates context for ongoing and recurring customer issues
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: Migrating an outdated knowledge base without updating it transfers inaccurate information to the new system
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: No customer communication plan means customers will encounter changed processes without warning
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: 6-week timeline for a multi-channel consolidation with cross-training is unrealistically aggressive
[PLACEHOLDER] Issue: The new ticketing platform login page should support dark mode for agent comfort
